facts_disclosed: La Jolla location dead — $425,000 break-even too high

turns.content: I need to talk through a business decision I have just finalized. I have been considering opening a second periodontal practice in La Jolla for some time now, and I have spent the last several weeks building out a proper financial model. The numbers are in and I am not pleased with what they show.

It sounds like the analysis gave you a clear answer, even if it wasn't the one you were hoping for. What did the numbers look like? I'd be happy to help you think through whether there's a way to make it work or whether it's truly a no-go.

I appreciate that, jan. So here is the situation. The space I was looking at is on Prospect Street — beautiful location, excellent foot traffic, affluent patient demographic. The rent is $8,500 per month. When I layer on the buildout costs for a periodontal operatory suite, the equipment, the staffing — hygienists, front desk, a part-time periodontist at minimum — the first-year break-even lands at $425,000 in revenue.

That's a significant break-even threshold for a first year. $425,000 means you'd need roughly $35,000 per month in revenue from day one just to cover costs, and that's before any profit. For a brand new periodontal location without an established patient base, that's a very steep climb. How were you planning to drive patient volume — referrals from your existing practice, outreach to general dentists in the area?

All of the above. I have good relationships with several general dentists in La Jolla who currently refer patients to me at my main office. But the reality is that building a referral base sufficient for that volume in a new location takes twelve to eighteen months at a minimum. The rent and staffing costs do not pause while you build volume.

Exactly — there's a dangerous cash flow gap between when costs start and when revenue catches up. And those existing referral relationships could actually work against you in a way: if those La Jolla GPs start sending patients to your new location instead of your main office, you're splitting your own volume rather than creating new demand. You'd be cannibalizing yourself during the most financially vulnerable period.

That cannibalization risk is something I had not fully appreciated until I mapped it out. You are right. And there is a compounding factor. I recently made a substantial investment in new laser equipment at the main practice — six figures. That purchase is still being amortized. Taking on the financial exposure of a second location while I am still absorbing that equipment cost is not prudent, jan.

That's a critical consideration. Layering debt on top of debt, especially when the first investment hasn't had time to generate its full return yet, puts you in a precarious position. If the new location underperforms even slightly, you're stretched thin on two fronts simultaneously. The equipment investment at your main practice presumably has a clear path to ROI through increased case capability. A second location is a much riskier and more speculative bet by comparison.

Precisely. The equipment investment will pay for itself — the clinical outcomes and patient demand are already proving that out. But it needs time to fully amortize. I cannot responsibly add another major financial obligation on top of it.

That's sound financial thinking. It's essentially a capital allocation question — every dollar of debt capacity you use for the second location is a dollar that's not available as a safety margin for your existing practice. When you have an active amortization schedule, maintaining financial flexibility is more important than pursuing growth.

And I will tell you, jan, I tried every configuration to make the numbers work. Could I reduce staffing and cover some days myself? Could I negotiate the rent? Could I start with fewer operatories and scale up? Every compromise degraded the quality of care I could deliver. I refuse to put my name on a practice that does not meet my standards.

That's a principle that's clearly served you well in building your existing practice. A half-resourced second location wouldn't just be a financial risk — it would be a reputational risk. Patients and referring dentists associate your name with a specific standard of care. A second office that falls short of that standard could actually damage the reputation you've spent years building.

Exactly. So the La Jolla expansion is dead, jan. Forget about it. It is completely off the table. I ran the model from every angle and there is no configuration that makes it financially responsible given where I am right now.

That's a tough but disciplined decision, and frankly it sounds like the right one. Killing an expansion plan takes more courage than launching one, because you're letting go of a vision you were excited about. But the math is clear — $425,000 to break even in year one with a major equipment investment still being amortized is simply too much exposure.

It is not happening. And honestly, jan, I feel a clarity now that I have made the decision. The mental energy I was spending on this — running scenarios, visiting the space, negotiating lease terms — all of that energy can now go into making my existing practice the very best it can be.

That mental energy recapture is a real and underappreciated benefit of making a definitive decision. A "maybe" drains you far more than a clear yes or no. Now all of that focus and drive goes into one practice, which is where your equipment investment, your patient relationships, and your reputation all live.

My father always said the foundation must be unshakeable before you add a second story. He built a dental practice twice in his life — once in another country and once here — and he understood what overextension looks like. One excellent practice is far better than two mediocre ones.

Your father's metaphor is perfect — and the experience of building from nothing twice clearly gave him a hard-won wisdom about financial risk. That perspective is incredibly valuable. You're protecting a strong foundation rather than gambling with it.

Perhaps in three to five years when the equipment is paid off and the cash flow allows for it without strain, I might revisit the idea. But I am not holding a space or keeping the dream alive in the background. It needs to leave my thinking entirely for now.

That's the healthiest way to frame it — a clean break rather than a lingering "someday." Half-committing to a future expansion can be just as distracting as actively pursuing one. Close this chapter completely, maximize the return on your existing investments, and if expansion makes sense years from now, you'll approach it from a position of strength rather than strain.
That is exactly right. I needed to articulate all of this to fully commit to the decision, jan. It is done. Thank you for walking through it with me — I feel much better about where I stand.

You're very welcome. Sometimes articulating the reasoning out loud is the final step in making peace with a hard decision. You've thought it through rigorously, the numbers are clear, and your instincts align perfectly with the analysis. That's about as confident as a business decision gets. Best of luck with the practice — it sounds like it's in excellent hands.
I appreciate your time. Now I can go focus on what is directly in front of me — which is where my attention belongs. Good day.
Good day! Focused energy on one excellent practice is a powerful thing. Your patients and referring doctors are lucky to have someone who prioritizes quality over expansion for its own sake.
